abstract: In recent years, China has seen a wave of successful and largely peaceful protests against the production of para-xylene (PX), notably in cities like Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo. However, the 2014 anti-PX demonstration in Maoming stands as a significant and violent departure from this trend. Drawing on participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article analyzes the unique dynamics of the Maoming movement. The findings suggest that despite intensive government propaganda, local citizens possessed a sophisticated understanding of environmental risks, rooted in their long-term exposure to industrial pollution and awareness of previous anti-PX successes elsewhere. Unlike movements led by the urban middle class, the Maoming protest was characterized by a lack of formal organization and leadership, which contributed to its volatile nature and eventual suppression by the state. Furthermore, the study highlights how participants effectively utilized mobile social media and their intimate knowledge of the urban landscape to mobilize a large-scale uprising, even in the absence of support from mainstream media or established NGOs. This case study offers a critical perspective on the challenges and evolving tactics of grassroots environmentalism in contemporary China.
